### Club competition

#### North America

The NHL is the best attended and most popular ice hockey league in the world, and is among the [major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada"). The league's history began after Canada's National Hockey Association decided to disband in 1917; the result was the creation of the National Hockey League with four teams. The league expanded to the United States beginning in 1924 and had as many as 10 teams before contracting to six teams—known today as the [Original Six](/wiki/Original_Six "Original Six")—by 1942–43. In 1967, the NHL doubled in size to 12 teams, undertaking one of the greatest expansions in professional sports history. A few years later, in 1972, a new 12-team league, the [World Hockey Association](/wiki/World_Hockey_Association "World Hockey Association") (WHA), was formed and its ensuing rivalry with the NHL caused a rapid escalation in players' salaries. In 1979, the 17-team NHL merged with the WHA creating a 21-team league.[[64]](#cite_note-:02-64) By 2017, the NHL had expanded to 31 teams, and after a realignment in 2013, these teams were divided into two conferences and four divisions.[[65]](#cite_note-:12-65) The league expanded to 32 teams in 2021.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") (AHL) is the primary developmental professional league for players aspiring to enter the NHL. It comprises 31 teams from the United States and Canada. It is run as a [farm league](/wiki/Farm_team "Farm team") to the NHL, with the vast majority of AHL players under contract to an NHL team. The [ECHL](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL") (called the East Coast Hockey League before the 2003–04 season) is a mid-level minor league in the United States with a few players under contract to NHL or AHL teams.

As of 2019, there are three minor professional leagues with no NHL affiliations: the [Federal Prospects Hockey League](/wiki/Federal_Prospects_Hockey_League "Federal Prospects Hockey League") (FPHL), [Ligue Nord-Américaine de Hockey](/wiki/Ligue_Nord-Am%C3%A9ricaine_de_Hockey "Ligue Nord-Américaine de Hockey") (LNAH), and the [Southern Professional Hockey League](/wiki/Southern_Professional_Hockey_League "Southern Professional Hockey League") (SPHL).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/2007_Memorial_Cup_warmup.JPG/250px-2007_Memorial_Cup_warmup.JPG)](/wiki/File:2007_Memorial_Cup_warmup.JPG)

Pre-game warmups prior to a [Memorial Cup](/wiki/Memorial_Cup "Memorial Cup") game. The tourney serves as the championship for the major junior [Canadian Hockey League](/wiki/Canadian_Hockey_League "Canadian Hockey League").

[U Sports](/wiki/U_Sports "U Sports") ice hockey is the highest level of play at the Canadian university level under the auspices of U Sports, Canada's governing body for university sports. As these players compete at the university level, they are obligated to follow the rule of standard eligibility of five years.
In the United States especially, [college hockey](/wiki/College_hockey "College hockey") is popular and the best university teams compete in the annual [NCAA Men's Ice Hockey Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Ice_Hockey_Championship "NCAA Men's Ice Hockey Championship"). The [American Collegiate Hockey Association](/wiki/American_Collegiate_Hockey_Association "American Collegiate Hockey Association") is composed of college teams at the club level.

In Canada, the [Canadian Hockey League](/wiki/Canadian_Hockey_League "Canadian Hockey League") is an umbrella organization comprising three major junior leagues: the [Ontario Hockey League](/wiki/Ontario_Hockey_League "Ontario Hockey League"), the [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League"), and the [Quebec Maritimes Junior Hockey League](/wiki/Quebec_Major_Junior_Hockey_League "Quebec Major Junior Hockey League"). It attracts players from Canada, the United States, and Europe. The major junior players are considered amateurs as they are under 21-years-old and not paid a salary, rather a stipend, and play a schedule similar to a professional league. Typically, the [NHL drafts](/wiki/NHL_draft "NHL draft") many players directly from the major junior leagues. In the United States, the [United States Hockey League](/wiki/United_States_Hockey_League "United States Hockey League") (USHL) is the highest junior league. Players in this league are also amateur with players required to be under 21-years old, but do not get a stipend, which allows players to retain their eligibility for participation in [NCAA ice hockey](/wiki/College_ice_hockey#NCAA "College ice hockey").

The [Professional Women's Hockey League](/wiki/Professional_Women%27s_Hockey_League "Professional Women's Hockey League") is the highest level of club competition in women's hockey. It was founded in 2023 and debuted in 2024 with three teams in Canada and three in the United States.[[59]](#cite_note-auto-59)

#### Eurasia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Stamp-russia2016-hockey-gagarin-cup.png/250px-Stamp-russia2016-hockey-gagarin-cup.png)](/wiki/File:Stamp-russia2016-hockey-gagarin-cup.png)

A Russian stamp commemorating the [Gagarin Cup](/wiki/Gagarin_Cup "Gagarin Cup"), which is presented to the [KHL](/wiki/Kontinental_Hockey_League "Kontinental Hockey League")'s playoff champion. The KHL is the largest ice hockey league in Eurasia.

The Kontinental Hockey League (KHL) is the largest and most popular ice hockey league in Eurasia. The league is the direct successor to the [Russian Super League](/wiki/Russian_Super_League "Russian Super League"), which in turn was the successor to the [Soviet League](/wiki/Soviet_Championship_League "Soviet Championship League"), the history of which dates back to the Soviet adoption of ice hockey in the 1940s. The KHL was [launched in 2008](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_KHL_season "2008–09 KHL season") with clubs predominantly from Russia, but featuring teams from other [post-Soviet states](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states"). The league expanded beyond the former Soviet countries beginning in the [2011–12 season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_KHL_season "2011–12 KHL season"), with clubs in Croatia and Slovakia. The KHL currently comprises member clubs based in [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") (1), China (1), [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan") (1) and Russia (19) for a total of 22.

The second division of hockey in Eurasia is the [Supreme Hockey League](/wiki/Supreme_Hockey_League "Supreme Hockey League") (VHL). This league features 24 teams from Russia and 2 from Kazakhstan. This league is currently being converted to a farm league for the KHL, similarly to the AHL's function in relation to the NHL. The third division is the [Russian Hockey League](/wiki/Russian_Hockey_League "Russian Hockey League"), which features only teams from Russia. The [Asia League](/wiki/Asia_League_Ice_Hockey "Asia League Ice Hockey"), an international ice hockey league featuring clubs from China, Japan, South Korea, and the Russian Far East, is the successor to the [Japan Ice Hockey League](/wiki/Japan_Ice_Hockey_League "Japan Ice Hockey League").

The highest junior league in Eurasia is the [Junior Hockey League](/wiki/Junior_Hockey_League_(Russia) "Junior Hockey League (Russia)") (MHL). It features 32 teams from post-Soviet states, predominantly Russia. The second tier to this league is the [Junior Hockey League Championships](/wiki/Junior_Hockey_League_Championships "Junior Hockey League Championships") (MHL-B).

#### Europe

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Lineup_%285154107439%29.jpg/250px-Lineup_%285154107439%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lineup_(5154107439).jpg)

Players from the [ZSC Lions](/wiki/ZSC_Lions "ZSC Lions") line up prior to a game. The club plays in the Swiss [National League A](/wiki/National_League_A "National League A").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Talviklassikko_2011.jpg/250px-Talviklassikko_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Talviklassikko_2011.jpg)

Finnish clubs [Jokerit](/wiki/Jokerit "Jokerit") and [HIFK](/wiki/HIFK_Hockey "HIFK Hockey") during a 2011 outside game at the [Helsinki Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Helsinki_Olympic_Stadium "Helsinki Olympic Stadium").

Several countries in Europe have their own top professional senior leagues. Many future KHL and NHL players start or end their professional careers in these leagues. The National League A in Switzerland, Swedish Hockey League in Sweden, SM-liiga in Finland, and Czech Extraliga in the Czech Republic are all very popular in their respective countries.

Beginning in the 2014–15 season, the [Champions Hockey League](/wiki/Champions_Hockey_League "Champions Hockey League") was launched, a league consisting of first-tier teams from several European countries, running parallel to the teams' domestic leagues. The competition is meant to serve as a Europe-wide ice hockey club championship. The competition is a direct successor to the [European Trophy](/wiki/European_Trophy "European Trophy") and is related to the 2008–09 [tournament of the same name](/wiki/Champions_Hockey_League_(2008%E2%80%9309) "Champions Hockey League (2008–09)").

There are also several annual tournaments for clubs, held outside of league play. [Pre-season](/wiki/Season_(sports) "Season (sports)") tournaments include the European Trophy, [Tampere Cup](/wiki/Tampere_Cup "Tampere Cup") and the [Pajulahti Cup](/wiki/Pajulahti_Cup "Pajulahti Cup"). One of the oldest international ice hockey competition for clubs is the [Spengler Cup](/wiki/Spengler_Cup "Spengler Cup"), held every year in [Davos](/wiki/Davos "Davos"), Switzerland, between Christmas and New Year's Day. It was first awarded in 1923 to the Oxford University Ice Hockey Club. The [Memorial Cup](/wiki/Memorial_Cup "Memorial Cup"), a competition for junior-level (age 20 and under) clubs is held annually from a pool of junior championship teams in Canada and the United States.

International club competitions organized by the IIHF include the [Continental Cup](/wiki/IIHF_Continental_Cup "IIHF Continental Cup"), the [Victoria Cup](/wiki/Victoria_Cup_(ice_hockey) "Victoria Cup (ice hockey)") and the European Women's Champions Cup. The [World Junior Club Cup](/wiki/World_Junior_Club_Cup "World Junior Club Cup") is an annual tournament of [junior ice hockey](/wiki/Junior_ice_hockey "Junior ice hockey") clubs representing each of the top junior leagues.

#### Other regions

The [Australian Ice Hockey League](/wiki/Australian_Ice_Hockey_League "Australian Ice Hockey League") and [New Zealand Ice Hockey League](/wiki/New_Zealand_Ice_Hockey_League "New Zealand Ice Hockey League") are represented by nine and five teams respectively. As of 2012, the two top teams of the previous season from each league compete in the [Trans-Tasman Champions League](/wiki/Trans-Tasman_Champions_League "Trans-Tasman Champions League").

[Ice hockey in Africa](/wiki/Ice_hockey_in_Africa "Ice hockey in Africa") is a small but growing sport; while no African ice hockey playing nation has a domestic national league, there are several regional leagues in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").

### National team competitions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Ice_hockey_pictogram.svg/250px-Ice_hockey_pictogram.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ice_hockey_pictogram.svg)

Pictogram used to identify ice hockey at the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/OvechkinKuba2010WinterOlympics.jpg/250px-OvechkinKuba2010WinterOlympics.jpg)](/wiki/File:OvechkinKuba2010WinterOlympics.jpg)

[Alexander Ovechkin](/wiki/Alexander_Ovechkin "Alexander Ovechkin") of the [Russian men's hockey team](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team") moves the puck as [Czech Republic's](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team") [Filip Kuba](/wiki/Filip_Kuba "Filip Kuba") defends against him, during the 2010 Olympics.

Ice hockey has been [played](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games "Ice hockey at the Olympic Games") at the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") since 1924 (and was played at the [summer games in 1920](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1920 Summer Olympics")). Hockey is Canada's national winter sport, and Canadians are extremely passionate about the game. The nation has traditionally done very well at the Olympic Games, winning six of the first seven gold medals. By 1956, its amateur club teams and national teams could not compete with the teams of government-supported players from the Soviet Union. The USSR won all but two gold medals from [1956](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1956_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1956 Winter Olympics") to [1988](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1988_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1988 Winter Olympics"). The United States won its first gold medal in [1960](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1960_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1960 Winter Olympics"). On the way to winning the gold medal at the [1980 Lake Placid Olympics](/wiki/1980_Winter_Olympics "1980 Winter Olympics"), amateur US college players defeated the heavily favoured Soviet squad—an event known as the "[Miracle on Ice](/wiki/Miracle_on_Ice "Miracle on Ice")" in the United States. Restrictions on professional players were fully dropped at the 1988 games in Calgary. NHL agreed to participate ten years later. The 1998 Games saw the full participation of players from the NHL, which suspended operations during the Games and has done so in subsequent Games up until 2018. The [2010 games in Vancouver](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") were the first played in an NHL city since the inclusion of NHL players. The 2010 games were the first played on NHL-sized ice rinks, which are narrower than the IIHF standard.

National teams representing the member federations of the IIHF compete annually in the IIHF Ice Hockey World Championships. Teams are selected from the available players by the individual federations, without restriction on amateur or professional status. Since it is held in the spring, the tournament coincides with the annual NHL Stanley Cup playoffs and many of the top players are hence not available to participate in the tournament. Many of the NHL players who do play in the IIHF tournament come from teams eliminated before the playoffs or in the first round, and federations often hold open spots until the tournament to allow for players to join the tournament after their club team is eliminated. For many years, the tournament was an amateur-only tournament, but this restriction was removed, beginning in 1977.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/IIHF16WC_-_Game_FIN_v_BLR.jpg/250px-IIHF16WC_-_Game_FIN_v_BLR.jpg)](/wiki/File:IIHF16WC_-_Game_FIN_v_BLR.jpg)

Skaters from the [Finnish](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team") and [Belarusian men's ice hockey teams](/wiki/Belarus_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Belarus men's national ice hockey team") shortly after a [face-off](/wiki/Face-off "Face-off") during the [2016 IIHF World Championship](/wiki/2016_IIHF_World_Championship "2016 IIHF World Championship"). The IIHF is an annual national team tournament.

The [1972 Summit Series](/wiki/Summit_Series "Summit Series") and [1974 Summit Series](/wiki/1974_Summit_Series "1974 Summit Series"), two series pitting the best Canadian and [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet "Soviet") players without IIHF restrictions were major successes, and established a rivalry between Canada and the USSR. In the spirit of best-versus-best without restrictions on amateur or professional status, the series were followed by five [Canada Cup](/wiki/Canada_Cup_(ice_hockey) "Canada Cup (ice hockey)") tournaments, played in North America. Two NHL versus USSR series were also held: the 1979 Challenge Cup and [Rendez-vous '87](/wiki/Rendez-vous_%2787 "Rendez-vous '87"). The Canada Cup tournament later became the World Cup of Hockey, played in 1996, 2004 and 2016. The United States won in 1996 and Canada won in 2004 and 2016.

Since the initial women's world championships in 1990, there have been fifteen tournaments.[[46]](#cite_note-IIHF_WWC_list-46) Women's hockey has been played at the Olympics since 1998.[[44]](#cite_note-Hunter_1998_Olympics-44) The only finals in the women's world championship or Olympics that did not involve both Canada and the United States were the [2006 Winter Olympic final](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2006 Winter Olympics – Women's tournament") between Canada and Sweden and [2019 World Championship final](/wiki/2019_IIHF_Women%27s_World_Championship_final "2019 IIHF Women's World Championship final") between the US and Finland.

Other ice hockey tournaments featuring national teams include the [World Junior Championship](/wiki/IIHF_World_Junior_Championship "IIHF World Junior Championship"), the [World U18 Championships](/wiki/IIHF_World_U18_Championship "IIHF World U18 Championship"), the [World U-17 Hockey Challenge](/wiki/World_U-17_Hockey_Challenge "World U-17 Hockey Challenge"), the [World Junior A Challenge](/wiki/World_Junior_A_Challenge "World Junior A Challenge"), the [Ivan Hlinka Memorial Tournament](/wiki/Ivan_Hlinka_Memorial_Tournament "Ivan Hlinka Memorial Tournament"), the [World Women's U18 Championships](/wiki/IIHF_World_Women%27s_U18_Championships "IIHF World Women's U18 Championships") and the [4 Nations Cup](/wiki/4_Nations_Cup "4 Nations Cup"). The annual [Euro Hockey Tour](/wiki/Euro_Hockey_Tour "Euro Hockey Tour"), an unofficial European championship between the national men's teams of the Czech Republic, Finland, Russia and Sweden have been played since 1996–97.

### Attendance records

Main article: [List of ice hockey games with highest attendance](/wiki/List_of_ice_hockey_games_with_highest_attendance "List of ice hockey games with highest attendance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/TheBigChillattheBigHouse.JPG/250px-TheBigChillattheBigHouse.JPG)](/wiki/File:TheBigChillattheBigHouse.JPG)

[The Big Chill at the Big House](/wiki/The_Big_Chill_at_the_Big_House "The Big Chill at the Big House") was a collegiate ice hockey game played at [Michigan Stadium](/wiki/Michigan_Stadium "Michigan Stadium") in 2010. The game set the attendance record for ice hockey games.

The attendance record for an ice hockey game was set on December 11, 2010, when the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan")'s [men's ice hockey team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_men%27s_ice_hockey "Michigan Wolverines men's ice hockey") faced cross-state rival [Michigan State](/wiki/Michigan_State_Spartans_men%27s_ice_hockey "Michigan State Spartans men's ice hockey") in an event billed as "[The Big Chill at the Big House](/wiki/The_Big_Chill_at_the_Big_House "The Big Chill at the Big House")". The game was played at [Michigan's (American) football](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_football "Michigan Wolverines football") venue, [Michigan Stadium](/wiki/Michigan_Stadium "Michigan Stadium") in [Ann Arbor](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan"), with a capacity of 109,901 as of the [2010 football season](/wiki/2010_Michigan_Wolverines_football_team "2010 Michigan Wolverines football team"). When UM stopped sales to the public on May 6, 2010, with plans to reserve remaining tickets for students, over 100,000 tickets had been sold for the event.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Ultimately, a crowd announced by UM as 113,411, the largest in the stadium's history (including football), saw the homestanding Wolverines win 5–0. *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")*, using a count of ticketed fans who actually entered the stadium instead of UM's figure of tickets sold, announced a final figure of 104,173.[[68]](#cite_note-Janela-68)[[69]](#cite_note-Big_Chill_record-69)

The record was approached but not broken at the [2014 NHL Winter Classic](/wiki/2014_NHL_Winter_Classic "2014 NHL Winter Classic"), which also held at Michigan Stadium, with the [Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings") as the home team and the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs") as the opposing team with an announced crowd of 105,491. The record for an NHL Stanley Cup playoff game is 28,183, set on April 23, 1996, at the [Thunderdome](/wiki/Thunderdome_(Tampa) "Thunderdome (Tampa)") during a Tampa Bay Lightning – [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") game.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

The attendance record for a professional women's game was set on April 20, 2024, when a sold-out crowd of 21,105 people at the [Bell Centre](/wiki/Bell_Centre "Bell Centre") in Montreal watched a PWHL game between [Montreal](/wiki/PWHL_Montreal "PWHL Montreal") and [Toronto](/wiki/PWHL_Toronto "PWHL Toronto").[[71]](#cite_note-71)

## International status

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Nokia_Arenan_avajaiset_14.jpg/250px-Nokia_Arenan_avajaiset_14.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nokia_Arenan_avajaiset_14.jpg)

The match between rival teams [Ilves](/wiki/Ilves "Ilves") (yellow and green) and [Tappara](/wiki/Tappara "Tappara") (blue and orange) on December 3, 2021, at [Nokia Arena](/wiki/Nokia_Arena_(Tampere) "Nokia Arena (Tampere)") in [Tampere](/wiki/Tampere "Tampere"), Finland.

Ice hockey is most popular in [Canada](/wiki/Ice_hockey_in_Canada "Ice hockey in Canada"), [Germany](/wiki/Ice_hockey_in_Germany "Ice hockey in Germany"), [Austria](/wiki/Ice_hockey_in_Austria "Ice hockey in Austria"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Ice_hockey_in_Switzerland "Ice hockey in Switzerland"), Eastern Europe, Northern Europe, and the [United States](/wiki/Ice_hockey_in_the_United_States "Ice hockey in the United States"). Ice hockey is the official [national winter sport](/wiki/National_sport "National sport") of Canada.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

In addition, ice hockey is the most popular winter sport in [Austria](/wiki/Sport_in_Austria "Sport in Austria"), [Belarus](/wiki/Sport_in_Belarus "Sport in Belarus"), the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Sport_in_the_Czech_Republic "Sport in the Czech Republic"), [Finland](/wiki/Sport_in_Finland "Sport in Finland"), [Germany](/wiki/Sport_in_Germany "Sport in Germany"), [Latvia](/wiki/Sport_in_Latvia "Sport in Latvia"), [Norway](/wiki/Sport_in_Norway "Sport in Norway"), [Russia](/wiki/Sport_in_Russia "Sport in Russia"), [Slovakia](/wiki/Sport_in_Slovakia "Sport in Slovakia"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sport_in_Sweden "Sport in Sweden"), and [Switzerland](/wiki/Sport_in_Switzerland "Sport in Switzerland"). [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America")'s [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) is the highest level for men's ice hockey and the strongest professional ice hockey league in the world. The [Kontinental Hockey League](/wiki/Kontinental_Hockey_League "Kontinental Hockey League") (KHL) is the highest league in Russia and much of Eastern Europe.

The [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") (IIHF) is the formal governing body for international ice hockey, with the IIHF managing international tournaments and maintaining the [IIHF World Ranking](/wiki/IIHF_World_Ranking "IIHF World Ranking"). Worldwide, the International Ice Hockey Federation has 83 member national associations, comprising 60 full members, 22 associate members, and one affiliate member.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In international competitions, the national teams of six countries (the [Big Six](/wiki/Big_Six_(ice_hockey) "Big Six (ice hockey)")) predominate: [Canada](/wiki/Canada_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada men's national ice hockey team"), [Czechia](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Sweden men's national ice hockey team"), and [the United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States men's national ice hockey team"). Of the 69 medals awarded all-time in men's competition at the Olympics, only seven medals were not awarded to one of those countries (or two of their precursors: the Soviet Union for Russia, and Czechoslovakia for Czechia). In the annual [Ice Hockey World Championships](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships"), 177 of 201 medals have been awarded to the six nations; Canada has won the most gold medals. Teams outside the Big Six have won only nine medals in either competition since 1953.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

The [World Cup of Hockey](/wiki/World_Cup_of_Hockey "World Cup of Hockey") is organized by the National Hockey League and the [National Hockey League Players' Association](/wiki/National_Hockey_League_Players%27_Association "National Hockey League Players' Association") (NHLPA), unlike the annual World Championships and quadrennial Olympic tournament, both run by the International Ice Hockey Federation. World Cup games are played under NHL rules and not those of the IIHF, and the tournament occurs prior to the NHL pre-season, allowing for all NHL players to be available, unlike the World Championships, which overlaps with the NHL's [Stanley Cup playoffs](/wiki/Stanley_Cup_playoffs "Stanley Cup playoffs"). Furthermore, all twelve Women's Olympic and 36 [IIHF Women's World Championship](/wiki/IIHF_Women%27s_World_Championship "IIHF Women's World Championship") medals were awarded to one of the Big Six. The [Canadian national team](/wiki/Canada_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada women's national ice hockey team") or the [United States national team](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States women's national ice hockey team") have between them won every gold medal of either series.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## Variants

### Pond hockey

Main article: [Pond hockey](/wiki/Pond_hockey "Pond hockey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Pond_hockey-LacBeauport2010-b.JPG/250px-Pond_hockey-LacBeauport2010-b.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pond_hockey-LacBeauport2010-b.JPG)

A game of [pond hockey](/wiki/Pond_hockey "Pond hockey") being played in [Lac-Beauport, Quebec](/wiki/Lac-Beauport,_Quebec "Lac-Beauport, Quebec")

Pond hockey is a form of ice hockey played generally as pick-up[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] hockey on lakes, ponds and artificial outdoor rinks during the winter. Pond hockey is commonly referred to in hockey circles as shinny. Its rules differ from traditional hockey because there is no hitting and very little shooting, placing a greater emphasis on skating, stickhandling and passing abilities. Since 2002, the [World Pond Hockey Championship](/wiki/World_Pond_Hockey_Championship "World Pond Hockey Championship") has been played on Roulston Lake in [Tobique Valley](/wiki/Tobique_Valley "Tobique Valley"), New Brunswick, Canada.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Since 2006, the [US Pond Hockey Championships](/wiki/US_Pond_Hockey_Championships "US Pond Hockey Championships") have been played in Minneapolis, Minnesota, and the [Canadian National Pond Hockey Championships](/wiki/Canadian_National_Pond_Hockey_Championships "Canadian National Pond Hockey Championships") have been played in Huntsville, Ontario.

### Sledge hockey

Main article: [Sledge hockey](/wiki/Sledge_hockey "Sledge hockey")

Sledge hockey is an adaption of ice hockey designed for players who have a physical disability. Players are seated in [sleds](/wiki/Sled "Sled") and use a specialized hockey stick that also helps the player navigate on the ice. The sport was created in Sweden in the early 1960s and is played under similar rules to ice hockey.

## In popular culture

Main articles: [Ice hockey in popular culture](/wiki/Ice_hockey_in_popular_culture "Ice hockey in popular culture") and [List of films about ice hockey](/wiki/List_of_films_about_ice_hockey "List of films about ice hockey")

Ice hockey is the [official winter sport of Canada](/wiki/Sports_in_Canada#National_sports "Sports in Canada"). Ice hockey, partially because of its popularity as a major professional sport, has been a source of inspiration for numerous films, television episodes and songs in Canadian and American popular culture.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)
